page: Microsoft Azure E16-8ads_v7 is an x86_64 architecture server belonging to the Eadsv7 family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 9V45 processor, which contains 4 physical cores with 2 threads per core. The system is equipped with 128.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 16.0 GB per core. Local storage is bundled with a 945 GB disk, and the instance supports Premium Storage.

In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates top-tier single-core CPU performance, as evidenced by stress-ng single-core results and single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression, which all rank in the top 10%. Multi-core CPU performance and multi-threaded decompression fall into the average range. Memory bandwidth tests, including read-only small block cached operations at 878.94 GB/sec and large block uncached writes at 34.09 GB/sec, also place in the middle tier.

While the server excels in single-threaded execution, its multi-core capabilities and medium-model LLM prompt processing represent relative performance tradeoffs. The high memory density relative to the compute core count makes this configuration qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-heavy workloads that do not require massive parallel processing. This server is suited for database operations, caching layers, and memory-intensive enterprise applications.
description: Microsoft Azure E16-8ads_v7 is an x86_64 memory-optimized server within the Eadsv7 family, utilizing the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It is configured with 8 dedicated vCPUs from an AMD EPYC 9V45 processor, featuring 4 physical cores and 2 threads per core. The server is equipped with 128.0 GB of RAM, providing a high resource density of 16.0 GB per core, alongside 945 GB of local storage. Benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU performance and single-threaded compression speeds, while multi-core operations and memory bandwidth remain in the average tier. This high memory-to-vCPU ratio offers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-bound tasks that do not demand extensive parallel compute. Tradeoffs include weaker prompt processing speeds on medium-sized language models. This configuration is suited for database hosting, caching systems, and memory-intensive enterprise workloads.
